title: Śrī Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (Garland of 108 Tīrthas)
summary: The Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā is a long devotional hymn (stavan) that takes the reciter on a 'pilgrimage in words', bowing in turn to 108 sacred Jain tīrthas — pilgrimage places — together with the Tīrthaṅkaras and eternal images enshrined across the universe. It opens with praise of the omniscient Jina, recalls the Tīrthaṅkaras of the past, present and future, salutes the eternal shrines of the three worlds, then names famous holy sites one by one (Aṣṭāpada, Girnar, Sammeta Śikhara, Śatruñjaya, Mount Ābu and many more), each with a few words of its story. Reciting it lets a worshipper venerate every Jain holy place at once, without leaving the prayer hall, and renew the longing for liberation that pilgrimage expresses.
ritualPlacement: This is a stavan (devotional hymn), not one of the six Āvaśyakas. It belongs to the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, where — per the recitation tree of this Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book — it follows the extended confession (Bṛhad Atiyār) and precedes the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys, the Nine Smaraṇas and the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because the Cāumāsī (Cāturmāsika) and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas have no independent text and are performed 'as per the Pakkhī' (with the word-substitutions 'pakkhī' → 'cāumāsī' / 'saṁvatsarī' and the longer kāyotsarga), this hymn is recited in those two observances as well. Its own verses contain no 'pakkhī' word to swap, so it is carried over unchanged.
authorAndSource: Composed by Ācārya Mahendrasiṁhasūri (Mahendrasinhasūri) of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha Gaccha), traditionally remembered as a disciple of Jayasiṁhasūri and active in the 13th century CE. The text is taken verbatim from the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition of *Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit* (પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત), the single golden source for this corpus. The hymn is in Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa-coloured gāthā verse. (Exact date of composition is not fixed by a verified colophon; see notes.)
structureArc: The 111 verses move from the universal to the particular and back to the universal. vv.1–11 open with doxology: acclamation (jaya jaya) of the omniscient Jina, the 'dhanno' verses praising the rare blessing of human birth and of standing at the Jina's feet, and the standard portrait of the Jina free of the 18 faults, adorned with 34 atiśayas and the 8 mahā-prātihāryas, conqueror of attachment, aversion and delusion. vv.12–15 salute the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras of the past cycle (atīta) in Bharata; vv.16–18 the present 24 (vartamāna), Ṛṣabha through Mahāvīra; vv.19–22 the 24 of the future cycle (anāgata). vv.23–40 turn to the eternal (śāśvata) images and shrines of the three worlds — enumerating caityas and pratimās across the vāsahara mountains, gajadanta and kuru-trees, Meru, Nandīśvara, Kuṇḍala and Rucaka, and the bhavanapati, vyantara, jyotiṣka and vaimānika realms — name the four eternal Jinas (Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vārisheṇa, v.33), and salute the 20 currently-living viharamāṇa Jinas led by Sīmandhara (vv.34–39), closing with an all-time salutation (v.40). vv.41–69 narrate the great legend-bearing tīrthas: Aṣṭāpada (Ṛṣabha's nirvāṇa, vv.41–45), Ujjayanta/Raivatagiri = Girnar (Nemi, vv.46–48), Daśārṇakūṭa (Indra humbling Daśārṇabhadra's pride before Mahāvīra, vv.49–55), Takṣaśilā Dharma-cakra tīrtha (vv.56–58), Ahicchatra (Pārśva sheltered by Dharaṇendra, vv.59–63), Rathāvarta and Kuñjarāvarta (Vajrasvāmī, vv.64–66) and Camarutpāta at Susumārapura (vv.67–69). vv.70–104 are a sweeping geographic roll-call of named sites — Sammeta Śikhara (vv.70–71), Vimalagiri/Śatruñjaya (Puṇḍarīka and the Pāṇḍavas, vv.72–75), the Mathurā Supārśva stūpa (v.76), Bharukaccha's Aśvāvabodha and Samalikā-vihāra of Munisuvrata (vv.77–80), Stambhanapura Pārśva (v.81), and a long sequence of Pārśva, Nemi, Vīra, Ṛṣabha and Candraprabha shrines through many towns and regions, including Mount Ābu (the Vimala-vasahī founded by Vimala and the second temple by Vastupāla, vv.92–96), Muṇḍasthala (vv.97–99), Tāraṇagiri (v.100) and Vāyaṭanagara (v.101), ending in a catalogue of cities and lands (vv.102–104). vv.105–111 universalise: homage to all images across Jambūdvīpa, Dhātakīkhaṇḍa and Puṣkarārdha and their 170 vijayas (v.105), to every image seen or unseen, known or unknown, of every material and size (vv.106–107), to all Tīrthaṅkaras of the three times (v.108), to every eternal tīrtha of the three worlds (v.109) and to the very lands where Jinas' birth, renunciation, omniscience, liberation and samavasaraṇa occurred (v.110), sealed by a colophon naming Mahendra (v.111).
recurringImagery: Three motifs recur and are explained here once so the per-verse commentaries need not re-gloss them. (1) The pilgrimage gesture itself: nearly every verse is built on a verb of homage — namimo / vande / thuṇimo / paṇamāmi ('I bow', 'I praise', 'I salute') — so the whole hymn reads as a single continuous act of reverence, a 'garland' (mālā) strung one tīrtha-bead at a time. (2) The crossing imagery behind the word tīrtha ('ford, crossing-place'): the world of rebirth is the ocean (bhava-jaladhi / saṁsāra), and the Jina and his tīrthas are repeatedly the means of crossing it — the boat or raft (taraṇḍa, pota, bohittha), the helmsman (nijjāmaga), the island (dīva) and the lamp (païva) amid the waters, and the wish-granting marvels that fulfil every need: the wish-tree (suratarū), the wish-cow (surabhi/kāmadhenu) and the wish-gem (cintāmaṇi). These cluster in the opening verses and again at Girnar (vv.46–48) and Bharukaccha. (3) The fourfold sweep over time and permanence: the Tīrthaṅkaras are saluted as past (atīta), present (vartamāna) and future (anāgata), and the images as eternal (śāśvata) versus non-eternal (a-śāśvata, the historically founded shrines), with the 20 viharamāṇa Jinas of Mahāvideha as the living, presently-teaching Jinas — a structure that lets the hymn claim to honour every Jina and every shrine that ever was, is, or will be. The Jina's fixed epithet-cluster — free of 18 doṣas, endowed with 34 atiśayas and 8 mahā-prātihāryas, conqueror of rāga, dveṣa and moha, destroyer of the 8 karmas — is established in vv.10–11 and assumed thereafter.
keyThemes: Veneration of the Tīrthaṅkaras of past, present and future
Eternal (śāśvata) images and shrines of the three worlds
The 20 living viharamāṇa Jinas of Mahāvideha
Tīrtha as the crossing of the ocean of rebirth
Sacred geography and the legends attached to each site
Universal homage culminating in the lands of Jina-events

glossary.definition: A 'crossing-place' — a sacred Jain pilgrimage site; figuratively, the means of crossing the ocean of rebirth. The hymn bows to 108 of them.
A 'ford-maker', a fully awakened Jina who re-establishes the fourfold community and the path; twenty-four arise in each half time-cycle.
'One hundred and eight' (lit. 'a hundred plus eight'); here the auspicious count of tīrthas garlanded in the hymn.
A 'garland of tīrthas' — a poetic genre that strings sacred sites together as a verbal pilgrimage.
An eternal, beginningless Jina image enshrined in the heavens and cosmic mountains, contrasted with historically founded (non-eternal) shrines.
The 'wandering', currently-living Tīrthaṅkaras — the 20 Jinas presently teaching in the Mahāvideha regions, led by Sīmandhara.
Past, present and future — the three time-frames in which the hymn salutes the successive sets of 24 Tīrthaṅkaras.
A Jain shrine or temple housing a Jina image; the hymn counts caityas across every region of the cosmos.
The divinely fashioned preaching assembly-hall in which a Tīrthaṅkara delivers his first sermon after attaining omniscience.
The eight great miraculous attributes (e.g. aśoka tree, divine throne, halo, drum) that accompany a Tīrthaṅkara; named among the Jina's standard adornments.
The 34 supernatural excellences of a Tīrthaṅkara's person and presence; part of the Jina's fixed descriptive cluster.
The 'ocean of worldly existence' (saṁsāra); the Jina and his tīrthas are the boat, raft and helmsman by which it is crossed.
The wish-fulfilling gem; with the wish-tree (suratarū) and wish-cow, a stock image for the boundless boon of meeting the Jina.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this as a guided armchair pilgrimage: notice the steady refrain of bowing words and let the place-names and their short stories wash past without trying to memorise the cosmic counts. An advanced practitioner should track the deliberate architecture — universal Jinas (past/present/future/eternal/viharamāṇa) framing a named, often regionally specific roll-call of tīrthas, returning to the universal — and may cross-check the geography and legends (Aṣṭāpada, Girnar, Sammeta Śikhara, Śatruñjaya, Mount Ābu) and the enumerations of śāśvata images against the cosmographic sources.

sources.title: Anchalgacchna Aetihasik Lekho (book summary), JainGPT
sources.consultedFor: Anchalgachchha lineage context and the difficulty of fixing an exact colophon date for Mahendrasiṁhasūri's tīrthamālā

notes: Author attribution to Ācārya Mahendrasiṁhasūri of the Anchalgachchha follows the authoritative tradition knowledge base and the source book; he is traditionally placed in the 13th century CE as a disciple of Jayasiṁhasūri, but no verified colophon fixing the exact year of composition was located, so the date is given as approximate. The text reproduces the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition verbatim, including its Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa orthography and internal spelling variants (e.g. 'atiyār' spelt અતિયાર), which should not be normalised. The traditional count is 108 (aṣṭottarī) tīrthas; the recitation comprises 111 numbered verses because several verses give framing, enumeration or multi-site catalogues rather than a single named tīrtha each — the headline number refers to the tīrthas honoured, not the verse total. Identifications of some sites (e.g. Pārkar/Gufāgiri, Muṇḍasthala, Tāraṇagiri, Vāyaṭanagara) reflect medieval western-Indian sacred geography and may carry variant readings in other editions.
